bytes.topic: Architecture & Structure — feature-based folder structure, colocation, barrel exports
bytes.title: Svelte Feature Folders — Colocation, Barrel Exports, and the $lib Alias
bytes.body:
Feature-based structure in SvelteKit means organizing under `src/lib/features/` by domain, not by type. Each feature owns its `.svelte` components, stores, server utilities, and types — colocated in one directory.

A barrel `index.ts` per feature exposes only its public surface. SvelteKit's `$lib` alias then makes imports clean and refactor-safe: `import { AuthForm, authStore } from '$lib/features/auth'`.

Keep `src/routes/` for routing shells only. `+page.svelte` files should import from feature libs — no business logic, no store declarations directly in route files. This keeps pages thin and features portable.

With Svelte 5, rune-based state lives in `.svelte.ts` files (not plain `.ts`), because `$state` and `$derived` are compile-time transforms. Name them `auth.store.svelte.ts` and barrel-export them the same way — the consumer never needs to care about the file extension.

Avoid god stores in `src/lib/stores.ts`. Colocated stores per feature prevent unrelated reactive dependencies from coupling across the app, and tree-shaking eliminates unused slices automatically.

bytes.topic: Component Design — single responsibility, 150-line rule, presentation vs logic separation
bytes.title: Svelte Component Design — Splitting Logic from Presentation with the 150-Line Rule
bytes.body:
Svelte's single-file format makes it tempting to pile reactive declarations, fetch calls, and markup into one `.svelte` file. Resist it.

The 150-line rule is a practical heuristic: if your file exceeds it, you have multiple responsibilities tangled together. Split aggressively.

Presentation components own zero state beyond transient UI state (open/closed, hovered). They receive data via `export let` props and communicate up via `createEventDispatcher`. No `fetch`, no store writes, no `onMount` side effects—purely declarative.

Logic lives elsewhere: SvelteKit's `load()` functions own route data fetching, `writable`/`derived` stores own shared mutable state, and plain `.ts` modules own pure computation. A `userStore.ts` exporting a `derived` readable is far more testable than a component juggling both concerns.

The tell: if your `<script>` block imports multiple stores, dispatches events, *and* runs `onMount` side effects, it's coordinating too much. Extract that orchestration into a parent container or store, leaving leaf components purely presentational.

Reactive declarations (`$:`) inside a presentation component should only derive display values from props—never trigger network calls or mutate external stores.
bytes.code_snippet:
{#if loading}Loading… {:else}

{user.name}

{/if}

bytes.topic: State Management — local vs lifted vs context vs external store, when to use each
bytes.title: Svelte State Management — Local, Lifted, Context, and Stores
bytes.body:
Svelte gives you four distinct layers for state, each with a clear use case. Use `let` for component-local state — it's reactive by default, no boilerplate needed. When a child needs to mutate parent state, lift it up and pass a callback or use `bind:value` for two-way binding, but keep this shallow — deep binding chains become brittle fast.

For cross-tree sharing without prop-drilling, `setContext`/`getContext` scopes state to a component subtree. It's synchronous and non-reactive on its own — pair it with a `writable` store to make it reactive. Crucially, context is per-component-instance, not global, so it's safe for things like a modal's internal state tree.

For truly global, reactive, shared state — user session, theme, cart — reach for Svelte stores: `writable`, `readable`, or `derived`. Subscribe with the `$` prefix in templates for automatic cleanup. `derived` is underused: it computes values from other stores lazily, replacing selectors you'd write manually in Redux. Avoid stores for ephemeral UI state — that's what `let` is for.
bytes.code_snippet:
import { writable, derived } from 'svelte/store'; export const cart = writable([]); export const cartCount = derived(cart, $cart => $cart.length);

{$cartCount} items

bytes.topic: Performance — memoization (useMemo/useCallback/computed), when it helps vs hurts
bytes.title: Svelte's $derived — When Memoization Helps and When It's Overkill
bytes.body:
Svelte 5 uses `$derived` (and `$derived.by` for complex logic) as its memoization primitive — it caches a computed value and only recomputes when its reactive dependencies change. Unlike React's `useMemo`, you don't manually declare a dependency array; Svelte's compiler tracks them automatically.

Where it pays off: expensive computations over large arrays, filtered/sorted lists derived from state, or values read in multiple places. Without `$derived`, each read would re-execute the expression on every render pass.

Where it hurts: wrapping trivial expressions like `$derived(count + 1)` adds overhead — the proxy tracking and cache lookup cost more than just computing inline. Similarly, `$derived` on a value used only once in the template offers zero benefit.

In Svelte 4, `$: computed = heavyFn(data)` was the equivalent — reactive statements memoized by the compiler. Svelte 5's `$derived` makes the intent explicit and composes better in class-based or `.svelte.ts` rune files. Reach for it when the computation is non-trivial or the derived value fans out to multiple consumers.

bytes.topic: Data Fetching — server state vs client state, TanStack Query patterns
bytes.title: Svelte Data Fetching — Server State vs Client State with TanStack Query
bytes.body:
Svelte's native `writable` stores handle client state well — local UI toggles, form values, ephemeral selections. But server state is fundamentally different: it's async, potentially stale, shared across components, and needs cache invalidation. Reaching for a `writable` for fetched data means manually wiring `isLoading`, error flags, and cache lifetimes.

`@tanstack/svelte-query` fills this gap. Unlike React's `useQuery`, `createQuery` returns a **Svelte store** — prefix with `$` in templates and it composes cleanly with `$:` reactive statements. Pass a `queryKey` array for cache identity and a `queryFn` for the fetch logic. The default stale-while-revalidate behavior renders cached data instantly, then silently refetches in the background.

Tune `staleTime` to control freshness windows — 5 minutes for quasi-static data, 0 for real-time feeds. For writes, `createMutation` exposes `.mutate()` and an `onSuccess` callback where you call `queryClient.invalidateQueries({ queryKey: ['posts'] })` to bust the cache.

Rule of thumb: `$state` (Svelte 5) or `writable` for local UI state; TanStack Query for anything that lives on a server. Conflating the two leads to stale renders and redundant fetches.
bytes.code_snippet:
{#if $posts.isPending}

Loading...

{:else if $posts.isError}

Error: {$posts.error.message}

{:else} {#each $posts.data as post (post.id)}

{post.title}

{/each} {/if}

bytes.topic: Forms — controlled vs uncontrolled, React Hook Form + Zod pattern
bytes.title: Svelte Forms — bind:value vs Uncontrolled Fields, and Superforms + Zod
bytes.body:
React's controlled vs uncontrolled distinction doesn't map cleanly to Svelte. `bind:value` is two-way binding by default — there's no `useState` overhead, no synthetic event wiring. 'Uncontrolled' in Svelte means reading from `FormData` directly in a `+page.server.ts` action, skipping reactive state entirely. That's the right call for simple forms: let the browser own the DOM, parse on submit.

For complex forms with validation, `sveltekit-superforms` + Zod is the idiomatic Svelte equivalent of React Hook Form + Zod. Define a Zod schema once, run `superValidate` on the server in both `load` and the action, then call `superForm(data.form)` in your component. It returns a `$form` store (bound to inputs), an `$errors` store (per-field Zod messages), and an `enhance` action that handles progressive enhancement. No client-side schema duplication — the same Zod schema validates on both sides. Constraint API (`$constraints`) auto-populates `min`, `maxlength`, and `required` attributes, cutting boilerplate further.

bytes.topic: TypeScript — prop typing, discriminated unions for variants, no any
bytes.title: Svelte 5 — Prop Typing with Discriminated Unions for Variant Components
bytes.body:
Svelte 5's `$props()` rune works seamlessly with TypeScript interfaces, giving you full type safety at the component boundary. For variant-style components — buttons, alerts, badges — discriminated unions eliminate the `any` escape hatch and make illegal states unrepresentable.

Define a union type where a shared `variant` literal field acts as the discriminant. TypeScript narrows the type automatically based on which variant is passed, so a `destructive` button can require a `confirmLabel` prop that a `default` button never needs. No runtime checks required — the compiler enforces it.

In Svelte 5, declare props with `let { variant, ...rest }: ButtonProps = $props()`. The destructuring is typed end-to-end: the parent gets a compile error if it passes a prop invalid for the selected variant. Avoid `type Props = { variant: string; label?: string }` — that's just a typed `any`. Use the union, let the discriminant do the work, and keep your component API honest.
bytes.code_snippet:
<!-- Button.svelte -->
<script lang="ts">
  type DefaultProps = { variant: 'default'; label: string };
  type DestructiveProps = { variant: 'destructive'; label: string; confirmLabel: string };
  type ButtonProps = DefaultProps | DestructiveProps;

  let props: ButtonProps = $props();
</script>

{#if props.variant === 'destructive'}
  <button>{props.label} — confirm: {props.confirmLabel}</button>
{:else}
  <button>{props.label}</button>
{/if}

bytes.topic: Error Handling — error boundaries per route, handle loading/error/empty states
bytes.title: Svelte Error Handling — Route Boundaries, Loading, and Empty States
bytes.body:
SvelteKit gives you per-route error boundaries via `+error.svelte` files. Drop one next to any `+page.svelte` and it catches errors thrown during `load` for that route and its children. Use the `error()` helper from `@sveltejs/kit` to throw typed HTTP errors — it sets both status and message, which `$page.error` and `$page.status` expose in your error page.

For loading states during client-side navigation, `$navigating` from `$app/stores` is your signal — it's non-null while a route transition is in flight. Avoid client-side `{#await}` for data that belongs in `load`; keep async work server-side so the page renders with resolved data.

Empty states live in `+page.svelte` itself. Since `load` returns typed data, a null or empty array check is straightforward — no ambiguity with loading state because SvelteKit serializes the distinction. Nest `+error.svelte` deeper in the route tree to scope error UI: an error in `src/routes/posts/[slug]/+page.ts` won't bubble to the root boundary if `posts/+error.svelte` exists.

bytes.topic: Testing — test behaviour not implementation, Testing Library philosophy
bytes.title: Svelte Testing — Test What Users See, Not How Signals Work
bytes.body:
Most Svelte component tests fail the wrong way: they assert on internal `$state` values or implementation details that users never interact with. The Testing Library philosophy flips this — query the DOM the way a user would, not through component internals.

With `@testing-library/svelte`, avoid reaching into component instances or inspecting reactive variables directly. Instead, query by role, label, or text: `getByRole('button', { name: /submit/i })` is robust to refactors; `component.loading` is not.

This matters in Svelte 5 especially. Runes (`$state`, `$derived`) are implementation details. If you refactor from `$derived` to a `$effect` that writes to `$state`, your UI behavior shouldn't change — and your tests shouldn't break if they're written correctly.

Fire real DOM events with `fireEvent` or `userEvent`, then assert on rendered output: visible text, ARIA state, disabled attributes. A button becoming disabled after a click is observable behavior. The boolean that caused it is not. Write tests that would still pass after a complete internal rewrite.

bytes.topic: Security — dangerouslySetInnerHTML, token storage, input sanitization
bytes.title: Svelte Security — {@html}, Token Storage, and Input Sanitization
bytes.body:
Svelte's {@html} tag is the direct equivalent of React's dangerouslySetInnerHTML — and equally dangerous. Passing unsanitized user content through it opens XSS vectors immediately. Always pipe through DOMPurify before rendering: {@html DOMPurify.sanitize(userContent)}. Never skip this for markdown renderers or comment systems.

For token storage, avoid localStorage for JWTs in Svelte apps. Scripts injected via XSS can trivially read localStorage. Prefer httpOnly cookies managed server-side. If you use SvelteKit, store session tokens in the server-side load() context and pass only non-sensitive derived state to the client via $page.data.

For form inputs, Svelte's two-way binding (bind:value) doesn't sanitize on its own — it just syncs the DOM value. Validate and sanitize in your form submission handler or in a SvelteKit form action using a schema library like Zod. In +page.server.ts actions, treat every field.get() value as untrusted. Svelte's reactivity model makes it easy to forget that $derived and $state are just JS — they carry whatever data you put in.
bytes.code_snippet:
<!-- XSS-safe HTML rendering in Svelte -->
<script lang="ts">
  import DOMPurify from 'dompurify';

  let { rawHtml }: { rawHtml: string } = $props();
  const safeHtml = $derived(DOMPurify.sanitize(rawHtml));
</script>

{@html safeHtml}

<!-- SvelteKit form action — treat all input as untrusted -->
// +page.server.ts
export const actions = {
  default: async ({ request }) => {
    const data = await request.formData();
    const comment = z.string().max(500).parse(data.get('comment'));
    // safe to persist
  }
};

bytes.topic: Code Quality — ESLint react-hooks plugin, naming conventions, 50-line rule
bytes.title: Svelte Code Quality — eslint-plugin-svelte, Naming Conventions, and the 50-Line Rule
bytes.body: Svelte has no react-hooks plugin to worry about, but eslint-plugin-svelte fills the gap with rules purpose-built for reactive code. Enable `svelte/no-unused-svelte-ignore`, `svelte/valid-compile`, and `svelte/no-reactive-reassign` — the last one catches the classic mistake of reassigning a `$:`-derived variable and expecting reactivity to follow. For naming, Svelte enforces its own grammar: component files must be PascalCase (`UserCard.svelte`), writable stores should use a noun (`userStore`, not `fetchUser`), and the `$` prefix is reserved for store auto-subscription — never prefix plain variables with it or you'll confuse the compiler. Event handlers live in the script block and follow `handleX` (e.g., `handleSubmit`), not inline arrow functions, which breaks reactivity tracing in Svelte 5 runes mode. Apply the 50-line rule to the `<script>` block specifically: if your script exceeds 50 lines, extract logic into a `.svelte.ts` module using `$state` and `$derived` runes. This keeps the component declarative and the logic independently testable.

bytes.topic: Accessibility — semantic HTML first, focus management, ARIA only when HTML falls short
bytes.title: Svelte Accessibility — Semantic HTML First, ARIA Only When HTML Falls Short
bytes.body:
Svelte's compiler warns you about missing ARIA attributes, but the real goal is avoiding ARIA entirely by reaching for native HTML first. A `<button>` beats `<div role="button">` every time — it's focusable, keyboard-operable, and announced correctly without a single line of JS.

For focus management, use Svelte's `bind:this` to capture DOM references and call `.focus()` imperatively after state transitions — e.g., returning focus to a trigger element when a modal closes. Wire this into `onMount` or reactive `$effect` blocks so it runs after the DOM settles.

Svelte 5's `$effect` is cleaner than `afterUpdate` for this: it runs post-DOM-commit and tracks dependencies automatically. Combine with `tick()` when you need to wait for a conditional block to render before focusing.

Use `aria-live="polite"` on dynamic regions (toast notifications, search results) where routing or reactive state swaps content the screen reader won't otherwise detect. Keep ARIA scoped — one `aria-live` region per concern, not a global wrapper.
bytes.code_snippet:
{#if dialogOpen} {/if}
{#if dialogOpen}Dialog is open{/if}

bytes.topic: Internationalization — never hardcode strings, react-i18next, Intl API for dates/numbers
bytes.title: Svelte i18n — Never Hardcode Strings, Use svelte-i18n and the Intl API
bytes.body:
Hardcoding strings in Svelte components is a maintenance trap—reach for `svelte-i18n` instead. It exposes a `$_` store that you import and call reactively; every locale switch triggers a re-render automatically because it's just a Svelte writable store under the hood.

Setup: call `register` and `init` once in your root `+layout.ts`, pointing to locale JSON files. Then in any component, `$_('key.path')` returns the translated string. Interpolation is `$_('greeting', { values: { name } })`.

For dates and numbers, skip library wrappers and drive `Intl.NumberFormat` and `Intl.DateTimeFormat` directly off `svelte-i18n`'s `$locale` store. A reactive declaration `$: formatter = new Intl.NumberFormat($locale, { style: 'currency', currency: 'USD' })` rebuilds whenever the locale changes—currency, percent, and compact notation are free. Same pattern for `Intl.DateTimeFormat` with `dateStyle: 'medium'`.

The key insight: `$_` and `$locale` are plain Svelte stores. Reactivity is automatic—no React context, no HOCs, no provider wrapping. Just subscribe with `$` and let the compiler handle the rest.
bytes.code_snippet:
{$_('welcome.title')}

{$_('item.price', { values: { name: 'Pro Plan' } })}

{currencyFormatter.format(price)}

{dateFormatter.format(date)}

bytes.topic: Web Vitals — LCP, CLS, INP and how component patterns affect each
bytes.title: Svelte and Core Web Vitals — How LCP, CLS, and INP React to Your Component Patterns
bytes.body:
Svelte's reactivity model touches all three Core Web Vitals in non-obvious ways.

LCP suffers when hero content sits behind an `{#await}` block — the browser can't discover the image until the promise resolves, delaying paint. Fix it by eagerly loading critical assets and injecting `<link rel="preload">` via `<svelte:head>` so the browser fetches them during HTML parse.

CLS is a common transition trap. Applying `transition:fly` or `transition:slide` to above-the-fold elements causes layout shifts before stable geometry exists. Reserve space with CSS `aspect-ratio` or explicit `width`/`height` attributes. When toggling visibility, prefer `visibility: hidden` over `{#if}` for layout-holding elements — `{#if}` removes nodes from the DOM, collapsing their space.

INP in Svelte 5 is a rune reactivity cost problem. A `$derived` that recomputes a large filtered list on every keystroke runs synchronously and blocks the interaction response. Use `await tick()` inside event handlers to yield control back to the browser mid-update, letting Svelte flush pending DOM writes before continuing expensive work.

bytes.topic: Memory Leaks — cleanup in useEffect/onUnmounted/onDestroy, AbortController, removeEventListener
bytes.title: Svelte Memory Leaks — Cleanup with $effect, AbortController, and removeEventListener
bytes.body:
Svelte's template event directives (`on:click`, `on:input`) clean up automatically. Leaks happen when you step outside the template — manual `addEventListener`, `fetch`, timers, or subscriptions.

In Svelte 5, `$effect` accepts a cleanup function as its return value. This runs before the effect re-executes and when the component is destroyed. Returning nothing means side effects live forever.

For `fetch`, wrap with `AbortController` and call `.abort()` in cleanup — this prevents stale responses from writing to `$state` after the component unmounts. For event listeners, store a named function reference so `removeEventListener` can match it exactly — anonymous functions never match.

In Svelte 4, use `onDestroy` from `'svelte'` for the same pattern. It's less ergonomic since cleanup lives separately from setup code, but the principle is identical.

One subtle trap: `$effect` in Svelte 5 re-runs when reactive dependencies change, so cleanup fires mid-lifecycle too — not just on destroy. Design your cleanup to be idempotent.
bytes.code_snippet:
<script lang="ts">
  let data = $state<unknown>(null);

  $effect(() => {
    const controller = new AbortController();

    fetch('/api/stream', { signal: controller.signal })
      .then(r => r.json())
      .then(d => (data = d))
      .catch(() => {}); // swallow AbortError

    const onKey = (e: KeyboardEvent) => console.log(e.key);
    window.addEventListener('keydown', onKey);

    return () => {
      controller.abort();
      window.removeEventListener('keydown', onKey);
    };
  });
</script>

bytes.topic: Real-time & WebSockets — singleton connection, reconnection logic, optimistic UI on send
bytes.title: Svelte WebSockets — Singleton Connection, Reconnection, and Optimistic UI with Runes
bytes.body:
A singleton WebSocket in Svelte 5 lives in a `.svelte.ts` module — a plain TypeScript file where runes are valid. Declare the socket and message state at module scope so every component importing the file shares the same connection instance, not one per mount.

For reconnection, attach an `onclose` handler that schedules `connect()` via `setTimeout`. Add exponential backoff by tracking retry count. Guard against infinite loops during HMR by checking `socket.readyState` before reconnecting.

Optimistic UI on send is clean with Svelte 5's deeply reactive `$state`: push the message as `{ text, confirmed: false }` before calling `socket.send()`. Because `$state` tracks mutations on arrays and objects, `messages.push()` triggers reactive updates automatically — no spread, no reassignment. When the server echoes back confirmation, find the pending entry by ID and flip `confirmed` to `true`.

Components import `messages` and `send` directly. Use `{#each messages as m}` to render. No per-component `onMount`/`onDestroy` socket wiring — the module handles lifecycle once at import time, keeping component code lean.

bytes.topic: Environment & Config — never hardcode URLs or keys, validate env vars at startup with Zod
bytes.title: SvelteKit Env Vars — Validate with Zod at Startup, Never at Runtime
bytes.body:
SvelteKit exposes environment variables through dedicated modules: `$env/static/private` for build-time server secrets, `$env/dynamic/private` for runtime server vars, and their `public` counterparts (prefixed with `PUBLIC_`) for client-safe values. Never reach for `process.env` directly — SvelteKit's module system enforces access boundaries and enables tree-shaking.

The weak point is silent failures: a missing `DATABASE_URL` won't crash until the first DB query. Fix this by validating all vars at startup with Zod. Create `src/lib/server/env.ts`, parse `$env/dynamic/private` through a Zod schema, and throw on failure. Then import that module in `hooks.server.ts` — SvelteKit runs this file before any request is handled, so misconfiguration surfaces immediately on boot, not in production at 3 AM.

Use `z.coerce.number()` for numeric vars (PORT, timeouts) since env values are always strings. Use `.default()` for optional config with sane fallbacks. Export the typed `config` object and import it wherever you need env values — no more scattered `env.DATABASE_URL` calls with silent `undefined` bugs.

bytes.topic: API Layer — single HTTP client instance, interceptors for auth/retry, never raw fetch in components
bytes.title: Svelte API Layer — One HTTP Client, Interceptors, Zero Raw Fetches in Components
bytes.body:
Svelte components should never call `fetch` directly. Scatter raw fetches across `.svelte` files and you lose centralized auth injection, retry logic, and error normalization overnight.

The pattern: one `$lib/api.ts` module exports a typed `api` object. Internally, a single `request()` function reads the auth token from a Svelte store via `get(authToken)` — synchronous, no subscription overhead — then attaches the `Authorization` header to every call. On a 401, the function retries once with the latest token value before surfacing an error.

In any `+page.svelte` or `load()` function, import `api` and call `api.get<T>('/bytes')`. The component stays clean. Token rotation, base URL config, and error shape all live in one file.

For SvelteKit `load()` functions running on the server, thread the `fetch` from `LoadEvent` into `request()` instead of the global — SvelteKit's `fetch` carries cookies and avoids port conflicts during SSR. Expose it as an optional parameter.

Result: zero raw fetches in components, automatic auth headers, and a single seam to attach OpenTelemetry spans or structured error logging.

bytes.topic: Animation — CSS for simple states, Framer Motion for complex, prefers-reduced-motion
bytes.title: Svelte Animation — CSS for State, Spring Stores for Motion, and prefers-reduced-motion
bytes.body:
Svelte's built-in primitives cover most animation needs without third-party libraries. For simple state changes — hover, focus, disabled — use CSS transitions via Svelte's `class:` directive or `style:` bindings. Zero JS overhead, no reactivity cost.

For complex motion, `svelte/motion` ships `tweened` (duration + easing) and `spring` (physics via `stiffness`/`damping`). Pair these with the `transition:` directive (`fly`, `fade`, `slide`, `crossfade`) for enter/exit choreography. Framer Motion is React-only — Motion One is the closest framework-agnostic alternative, but Svelte's built-ins handle 90% of cases.

Always gate animation intensity on `prefers-reduced-motion`. In Svelte 5, `MediaQuery` from `svelte/reactivity` gives a reactive, SSR-safe handle. Feed `reduced.current` into `tweened`'s `duration` and your `transition:` params — set both to `0` when reduced is active. Avoid duplicating conditional markup; keep the logic in the motion config itself. This makes `prefers-reduced-motion` a first-class citizen in your motion layer, not an afterthought.
bytes.code_snippet:
{#if visible}
Animated content
{/if}

bytes.topic: Global UI Patterns — toast system at app root, modal management, confirmation dialogs as hooks
bytes.title: Svelte Global UI Patterns — Toast, Modal, and Confirmation Dialogs Done Right
bytes.body:
Global UI like toasts and modals belongs in `+layout.svelte`, not scattered across routes. The idiomatic SvelteKit pattern: create module-level state that acts as a shared event bus, mount the UI at the root layout, call the functions anywhere.

For confirmation dialogs, go further — a promise-based approach turns `confirm()` into an awaitable call usable inside any event handler. Export an `ask(message)` function that stores the message alongside its `Promise` resolver. The `<ConfirmDialog>` mounted in your layout calls `respond(true|false)` on user action, which unblocks the awaiting caller. No component coupling, no callback props.

For toasts, a `$state` array in a `.svelte.ts` file with auto-dismiss via `setTimeout` is sufficient — push from any module, subscribe reactively in the layout.

Svelte 5's `.svelte.ts` rune files are the right home for this shared state: `$state` gives you fine-grained reactivity without writable store boilerplate, and the module is importable everywhere. Mount once in `+layout.svelte`, call from any route, action, or server response handler — zero prop drilling, zero context threading.

bytes.topic: Bundle & Asset Optimization — tree-shaking, lazy-load heavy components, analyze bundle
bytes.title: Svelte Bundle Optimization — Tree-Shaking, Lazy Loading, and Bundle Analysis
bytes.body: Svelte's compiler already eliminates unused component code at build time, but you still need to be deliberate about what ends up in your initial bundle. For heavy components — rich text editors, chart libraries, code highlighters — use dynamic imports with `{#await}` to defer loading until needed. Vite's `rollupOptions.output.manualChunks` lets you split vendor dependencies into separate chunks, keeping your entry bundle lean. To analyze what's actually shipping, install `rollup-plugin-visualizer` and add it to your `vite.config.ts` plugins array — it generates an interactive treemap of every module. For component-level lazy loading, wrap dynamic imports in a loading state using Svelte's `{#await}` block rather than reaching for a third-party suspense abstraction. Also audit your `$lib` barrel files — re-exporting everything from an `index.ts` can defeat tree-shaking if bundlers can't statically analyze the exports. Prefer direct imports (`import Chart from '$lib/Chart.svelte'`) over barrel re-exports for large, infrequently used components.

bytes.topic: Dev Tooling & Quality Gates — Prettier, Husky + lint-staged, tsc --noEmit in CI
bytes.title: Svelte Quality Gates — Prettier, Husky + lint-staged, and tsc --noEmit in CI
bytes.body:
Svelte projects need quality gates tuned to their toolchain. Prettier handles `.svelte` files via `prettier-plugin-svelte` — without it, the formatter mangles `<script>`, markup, and `<style>` blocks. Add it to your Prettier config and set `parser: 'svelte'` for `.svelte` overrides.

Husky + lint-staged runs checks only on staged files. Wire `svelte-check` (not just `tsc`) into lint-staged — it catches type errors inside `<script lang="ts">` blocks that plain `tsc` misses because `.svelte` files aren't TypeScript modules. Your `lint-staged` config should run `prettier --write` then `svelte-check --tsconfig ./tsconfig.json` on `*.svelte` patterns.

In CI, `tsc --noEmit` validates your non-Svelte TypeScript (utils, stores, hooks), while a separate `svelte-check` step covers component types. Splitting them gives clearer failure signals. Run both in parallel to keep pipelines fast. Skip `svelte-check` on the local hook if it's slow — gate it in CI only and use `--threshold error` to allow warnings without failing the build.

bytes.topic: Monitoring in Production — Sentry for errors, Web Vitals reporting, source maps
bytes.title: Svelte in Production — Sentry Error Capture, Web Vitals, and Source Maps
bytes.body:
`@sentry/sveltekit` provides SvelteKit-aware error capture through `hooks.client.ts`. Exporting `handleError = Sentry.handleErrorWithSentry()` catches unhandled errors that reach SvelteKit's error boundary — including failed `load` functions and uncaught promise rejections triggered during navigation.

Source maps wire up via `sentrySvelteKit()` added to the `plugins` array in `vite.config.ts`. The plugin auto-uploads maps during `vite build` using `SENTRY_AUTH_TOKEN` — no separate CLI upload step needed. Set `sourceMapsUploadOptions.org` and `.project` to match your Sentry project slug.

For Web Vitals, call `onCLS`, `onLCP`, and `onINP` from the `web-vitals` package inside `onMount` in your root `+layout.svelte`. Each callback fires when the browser finalises the metric; forward it to Sentry via `captureEvent` at `level: 'info'` to keep vitals out of your error quota. SvelteKit's client-side routing means CLS and LCP accumulate across soft navigations — tag each event with `$page.url.pathname` from `$app/stores` for per-route breakdown in Sentry dashboards.

bytes.topic: Reactivity Models — how React, Vue, Svelte, and Solid each decide what re-runs on state change
bytes.title: Svelte's Reactivity Model — Compiler-Driven, Not Runtime-Tracked
bytes.body:
Svelte's reactivity is fundamentally different from React, Vue, and Solid. React re-renders entire components on setState, relying on a virtual DOM diff. Vue uses runtime Proxy objects to intercept reads/writes, building a dependency graph at runtime. Solid is similar to Vue but more granular — signals track subscriptions at the expression level. Svelte does none of this at runtime.

The Svelte compiler statically analyzes your `.svelte` file and rewrites assignments into invalidation calls. When you write `count++`, the compiler emits `$$invalidate('count', count++)`. There's no Proxy, no virtual DOM, no subscription tracking at runtime — just direct DOM mutations keyed to specific variables.

This means reactivity in Svelte 4 is assignment-triggered: mutating an array with `.push()` won't update the DOM unless you follow it with a reassignment (`items = items`). Svelte 5 runes (`$state`, `$derived`) shift this to an explicit signal model — closer to Solid — but still compile away to lean, direct DOM updates. Understanding the compiler's role explains both Svelte's performance ceiling and its edge cases.
bytes.code_snippet:
<!-- Svelte 5: runes-based reactivity -->
<script>
  let count = $state(0);
  let doubled = $derived(count * 2);

  // $effect runs when its reactive deps change
  $effect(() => {
    console.log('count changed:', count);
  });
</script>

<button onclick={() => count++}>
  Count: {count} — Doubled: {doubled}
</button>
